title: How to Record Prepaid Rent Expense
description: A guide on recording the initial cash payment and the monthly adjusting entry for prepaid rent.
explanation: This records the 12-month advance payment. Every month, you will credit Prepaid Rent and debit Rent Expense for $100.

title: Recording Restaurant Sales via Talabat
description: How to account for gross sales, delivery commissions, and net cash received from delivery apps.
explanation: This entry ensures your revenue reflects the full sale amount while properly tracking the commission costs paid to the platform.

title: Journal Entry for Accrued Payroll
description: Recording wages earned by employees but not yet paid at the end of the accounting period.
explanation: This is a year-end/month-end adjustment to ensure expenses match the period in which the work was performed.

title: Annual SaaS Subscription Revenue
description: Recording a bulk annual payment for a software subscription under the accrual basis.
explanation: Since the service hasn't been provided yet, the payment is a liability. You recognize 1/12th as revenue each month.

title: Buying a Company Vehicle with a Loan
description: Recording a fixed asset purchase with a cash down payment and a bank loan.
explanation: This entry reflects the increase in assets (Vehicle) and the simultaneous decrease in cash and increase in debt.

title: Recording VAT Input Tax in Jordan
description: How to record Value Added Tax (VAT) on local purchases and expenses for Jordanian companies.
explanation: In Jordan, the standard Sales Tax is 16%. You debit the VAT Input account as an asset because it will be deducted from your VAT Output when filing with the ISTD.

title: Monthly SaaS Revenue Recognition
description: The adjusting entry to recognize monthly revenue from a previously collected annual subscription.
explanation: This follows the IFRS 15 framework. You are reducing the liability (Deferred Revenue) and 'earning' the revenue as the service is provided over time.

title: Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (Bad Debt)
description: Creating a provision for accounts receivable that may not be collected at year-end.
explanation: This is a key CMA concept. Using the allowance method adheres to the Matching Principle by recording the expense in the same period as the related sale.

title: Recording Restaurant Food Spoilage & Waste
description: How to adjust inventory for wasted, expired, or spoiled food items in a kitchen environment.
explanation: Regularly recording spoilage prevents your COGS from being artificially inflated and keeps your ending inventory balance accurate.

title: Bank Reconciliation: Interest Earned
description: Adjusting the general ledger for interest income reported on a bank statement.
explanation: This is a standard 'Book Side' adjustment during a bank reconciliation to ensure the GL matches the actual bank balance.

title: Recording Retention Money in Construction
description: How to account for the percentage of payment withheld by a client until project completion.
explanation: Retention is common in Jordan's construction sector. Even though you haven't received the cash, you recognize the full revenue and split the receivable into current and non-current (retention) portions.

title: Provision for Sales Returns (E-commerce)
description: Estimating and recording expected returns for online sales at the end of the month.
explanation: To follow the matching principle, you must estimate how much of this month's sales will be returned next month, especially for high-return industries like fashion e-commerce.

title: Replenishing the Petty Cash Fund
description: The entry required to record expenses paid out of petty cash and bring the fund back to its original balance.
explanation: Notice you do NOT credit Petty Cash itself unless you are reducing the fund size. You credit the main Bank account to replace the cash spent on specific expenses.

title: Sale of Fixed Asset at a Gain
description: How to remove an asset from the books and record a profit on the sale.
explanation: You must 'flush out' both the original cost and the accumulated depreciation to zero out the asset's book value before recording the gain.

title: Declaration of Cash Dividends
description: Recording the board's approval to pay dividends before the actual cash is sent.
explanation: The moment dividends are declared, they become a legal liability for the company, reducing the equity available for reinvestment.

title: Initial Recognition of Lease (IFRS 16)
description: Recording the Right-of-Use (ROU) asset and Lease Liability for a new office lease.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, almost all leases come onto the balance sheet. This entry represents the present value of future lease payments.

title: Investment in Equity Securities (FVTPL)
description: Recording the purchase of shares in another company held for trading.
explanation: FVTPL stands for 'Fair Value Through Profit or Loss'. Any change in share price at year-end will go directly to the income statement.

title: Inventory Write-Down to Net Realizable Value
description: Adjusting inventory value when the market price drops below the cost (LCM/NRV rule).
explanation: This follows the Conservatism principle. You must report inventory at the lower of cost or its net realizable value.

title: Adjusting Entry for Unearned Revenue
description: Recognizing revenue that was previously collected as an advance payment.
explanation: As the work is completed, you move the amount from the liability (Unearned) to the income statement (Revenue).

title: Issuance of Bonds at a Premium
description: Recording the cash received when bonds are sold for more than their face value.
explanation: A premium occurs when the bond's stated interest rate is higher than the market rate. The premium is amortized over the life of the bond.

title: Foreign Currency Exchange Gain
description: Recording a gain when settling a foreign currency invoice at a favorable rate.
explanation: Common in Jordan for imports. If the JOD/USD rate fluctuates in your favor between the invoice date and payment date, you record a gain.

title: Issuance of Common Stock above Par
description: Recording the sale of new shares to investors including Additional Paid-In Capital (APIC).

entries.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par

explanation: This shows how equity is split between the legal par value and the actual amount paid by investors.

title: Depreciation of Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset
description: Monthly depreciation entry for an office or equipment lease under IFRS 16 standards.
explanation: Even though you don't 'own' the building, IFRS 16 requires you to depreciate the Right-of-Use asset over the shorter of the lease term or the asset's useful life.

title: Purchase of Treasury Stock (Cost Method)
description: Recording the buyback of a company's own shares from the open market.
explanation: Treasury stock is not an asset; it is a contra-equity account. It reduces total shareholders' equity on the balance sheet.

title: Applying Manufacturing Overhead
description: Allocating indirect production costs to Work-in-Process (WIP) based on a predetermined rate.
explanation: This is a core CMA concept. Overhead is applied using a driver (like machine hours) to ensure products carry the full cost of production.

title: Impairment Loss on Goodwill
description: Recognizing a permanent decrease in the value of goodwill recorded during an acquisition.
explanation: Under IFRS/GAAP, goodwill is not amortized; instead, it is tested for impairment annually. If its value drops, it must be written down immediately.

title: Collection of Sales Tax (VAT Output)
description: Recording a sale that includes sales tax (VAT) collected from a customer on behalf of the government.
explanation: The tax collected is not your revenue; it is a liability you owe to the ISTD (in Jordan). You act as a middleman for the government.

title: Provision for Inventory Obsolescence
description: Recording a reserve for stock that is unlikely to be sold due to damage or lack of demand.
explanation: This follows the Prudence principle. Instead of reducing inventory directly, we create a 'bucket' (allowance) to show the estimated loss in value.

title: Accrual of Professional Audit Fees
description: Recognizing the expense for the annual audit before the invoice is received or paid.
explanation: Audits usually happen after year-end, but the work relates to the prior year. This entry ensures the expense hits the correct fiscal period.

title: Amortization of Prepaid Software Licenses
description: Monthly adjustment to recognize the expense of an upfront software subscription payment.
explanation: If you pay $2,400 for a yearly SAP or QuickBooks license, you record $2,400 as a prepaid asset and then 'bleed' $200 into expenses every month.

title: Accruing Intercompany Loan Interest
description: Recording interest earned on a loan provided to a subsidiary or related entity.
explanation: Common in groups like alfanar. Even if no cash moves, the interest must be recognized to reflect the arms-length transaction principle.

title: Sale of Manufacturing Scrap Metal
description: Recording the miscellaneous income from selling byproduct or waste materials from production.
explanation: If the scrap value is significant, it can be used to reduce the cost of the main product; otherwise, it is recorded as 'Other Income'.

title: Reconciling Payouts from Payment Processors
description: Recording the net cash received after processing fees from Stripe, PayPal, or HyperPay.
explanation: Never record just the net amount ($97) as revenue. You must record the full $100 sale to keep your tax reporting accurate.

title: Fixed Asset Revaluation (IFRS)
description: Increasing the carrying value of land or buildings to reflect current market fair value.
explanation: Under the IFRS Revaluation model, the gain does not go to the Income Statement; it goes to 'Other Comprehensive Income' (OCI) in Equity.

title: Bank Service Charges & Transfer Fees
description: Recording monthly maintenance or wire transfer fees deducted by the bank.
explanation: Usually discovered during the bank reconciliation process. This ensures the company's ledger matches the bank statement.

title: End of Service Benefit (EOSB) Provision
description: Accruing the liability for employee severance pay as required by Middle Eastern labor laws.
explanation: In Jordan and Saudi Arabia, companies must build a reserve for the gratuity pay employees receive when they leave.

title: Direct Write-Off of Uncollectible Account
description: Recording a loss when a specific customer's balance is confirmed as uncollectible.
explanation: This method is used when no prior allowance (provision) was made. It directly reduces the accounts receivable balance.

title: Transferring WIP to Finished Goods
description: Recording the completion of production and moving costs from Work-in-Process to Finished Goods inventory.
explanation: This represents the 'Cost of Goods Manufactured'. It moves all accumulated material, labor, and overhead into the final salable product.

title: Provision for Warranty Claims
description: Estimating and recording future warranty repair costs at the time of sale (Matching Principle).
explanation: You must record the expense in the same period as the sale, even if the actual repair happens months later.

title: Restricted Grant Revenue (Non-Profit)
description: Recording a donation that must be used for a specific project or timeframe.
explanation: In NGO accounting, restricted funds are tracked separately to ensure the donor's conditions are met before the funds are 'released' to general use.

title: Bond Interest with Discount Amortization
description: Recording periodic interest payment and the amortization of a bond discount using the straight-line method.
explanation: The interest expense is higher than the cash paid because it includes the 'cost' of the discount spread over the bond's life.

title: Cash Dividends Received on Investment
description: Recording income from dividends paid by a company in which you own shares (Cost Method).
explanation: Under the cost/fair value method, dividends are recorded directly as income when the right to receive payment is established.

title: Exchange of Assets (No Commercial Substance)
description: Trading an old machine for a similar new one where the future cash flows don't change significantly.
explanation: If an exchange lacks commercial substance, you generally do not recognize a gain; you simply carry over the book value to the new asset.

title: Capitalizing Internal Software Development
description: Recording internal labor costs as an intangible asset during the application development stage.
explanation: For SaaS companies, coding costs after reaching 'technical feasibility' are moved from the income statement to the balance sheet as an asset.

title: Prepaid Social Media Ad Campaign
description: Recording a large upfront payment for a 3-month marketing campaign on Meta or Google Ads.
explanation: Just like rent, advertising should be expensed as the ads are actually shown, not necessarily when the invoice is paid.

title: Sale of Construction Site Scrap
description: Recording revenue from selling excess rebar or copper scrap from a job site.
explanation: In Jordan's construction sector, scrap sales are often treated as 'Other Income' rather than a reduction of the project's material cost.

title: Unrealized Gain on Trading Securities
description: Adjusting the value of stocks held in a trading portfolio to fair market value at year-end.
explanation: Because these are 'Trading' securities, the gain is recorded on the Income Statement even if you haven't sold the shares yet.

title: Daily Talabat Sales Reconciliation
description: Recording the daily gross sales and the corresponding receivable from Talabat before payout.
explanation: This records the sales reported by the Talabat portal. The cash isn't in the bank yet, so it sits in a specific receivable account.

title: Talabat Commission & Service Fee
description: Recording the commission percentage deducted by Talabat on total orders.
explanation: Platform commissions (usually 20-30%) reduce the amount Talabat owes you. This entry recognizes the cost of using the service.

title: Talabat Weekly Bank Payout
description: Recording the actual cash transfer from Talabat to your Jordanian bank account.
explanation: This clears the receivable once the money is successfully transferred to your bank account.

title: Talabat Order Cancellation (Restaurant Fault)
description: How to record a refund when an order is cancelled after the food was prepared.
explanation: If the restaurant is at fault for a cancellation, Talabat will deduct the amount from your next payout.

title: Talabat Shared Marketing Promotion
description: Recording a discount where the restaurant pays 50% of a customer promotion.
explanation: When you run a 'Buy 1 Get 1' or % discount on the app, your shared portion of that cost is recorded as a marketing expense.

title: Talabat Monthly Tablet/Device Fee
description: Recording the monthly subscription or rental fee for the Talabat order terminal.
explanation: Often, platforms deduct a small hardware or software maintenance fee directly from your sales balance.

title: VAT on Talabat Commission (Jordan)
description: Recording the 16% Sales Tax applied to the commission invoice from Talabat.
explanation: In Jordan, the commission service is taxable. You can claim this 16% back against your sales tax output.

title: Talabat Disputed Payout Adjustment
description: Writing off a balance that Talabat refused to pay due to a technical dispute.
explanation: If a reconciliation error occurs and Talabat denies a claim, the uncollectible receivable must be written off.

title: Recording Delivery Fee Revenue (Own Fleet)
description: If the restaurant uses its own drivers but the Talabat app, recording the delivery fee collected.
explanation: This separates food revenue from delivery service revenue, which is important for margin analysis.

title: Driver Tips Collected via Talabat
description: Handling tips paid by customers on the app that must be passed to drivers.
explanation: Tips are not restaurant revenue. They are a liability until you pay them out to your delivery staff.

title: Recovery of Previously Written-Off Bad Debt
description: Recording cash received from a customer whose balance was already written off in a prior period.
explanation: When a 'dead' debt is suddenly paid, you recognize the cash and record it as other income or a reduction in bad debt expense.

title: Establishing a Petty Cash Fund
description: Setting up an initial float for small office expenses (Imprest System).
explanation: This moves cash from the main bank account to a physical drawer in the office. The balance stays at $200 unless the fund size is changed.

title: Zakat Provision (Saudi Arabia/GCC)
description: Accruing the estimated Zakat liability for a company operating in the GCC.
explanation: Zakat is calculated based on the Zakat base (equity, provisions, and long-term liabilities). This is essential for companies like alfanar.

title: Inventory Shrinkage (Theft/Error)
description: Adjusting inventory records after a physical count reveals a shortage compared to the system.
explanation: Shrinkage accounts for theft, breakage, or administrative errors that cause the physical stock to be lower than the books.

title: Accruing Monthly Utilities (Electricity/Water)
description: Recording estimated utility costs at month-end before the actual bill arrives.
explanation: To follow the accrual basis, you must estimate and record the cost of electricity used during the month, even if the bill comes later.

title: Purchase Returns and Allowances
description: Returning defective raw materials to a supplier for a full credit.
explanation: This reduces the amount you owe the vendor and adjusts your inventory balance for the returned items.

title: Issuance of Short-Term Note Payable
description: Borrowing cash from a bank by signing a formal 6-month promissory note.
explanation: Notes are different from Accounts Payable as they are formal legal documents and usually carry an explicit interest rate.

title: Recognition of Goodwill in Acquisition
description: Recording the excess price paid over the fair value of net assets when buying a business.
explanation: Goodwill represents intangible value like brand reputation and customer loyalty that isn't on the target company's books.

title: Payment of Previously Declared Dividends
description: The actual cash outflow to shareholders following the declaration date.
explanation: This clears the liability created during the 'Declaration' phase and records the cash leaving the company.

title: Monthly Amortization of Prepaid Insurance
description: The adjusting entry to move one month of coverage from assets to expenses.
explanation: Commonly checked in audits. If you paid for a 1-year policy, you must recognize 1/12th of that cost every month.

title: Receipt of Advance Rent (Unearned)
description: Recording cash received from a tenant for the next 6 months of rent.
explanation: Because you haven't 'provided' the space yet, the money is a liability. You owe the tenant the service (space) or their money back.

title: Remitting Sales Tax (VAT) to Authorities
description: Paying the net tax owed (Output - Input) to the tax department.
explanation: This is the final step in the VAT cycle—transferring the collected taxes from your books to the government.

title: Impairment of Machinery
description: Writing down an asset whose fair value has fallen below its book value permanently.
explanation: If a machine becomes obsolete or damaged, you must reduce its carrying value to reflect its actual worth.

title: Issuing a Salary Advance to Employee
description: Recording a short-term loan given to an employee against their next paycheck.
explanation: This is a receivable for the company. It will be deducted from the employee's salary at the end of the month.

title: Accruing Annual Employee Bonuses
description: Recording the expense for bonuses earned in the current year but paid in the next year.
explanation: Bonuses must be matched to the year the performance occurred, even if the check isn't cut until February.

title: Annual Software Subscription Renewal
description: Paying for another year of a mission-critical SaaS tool.
explanation: Just like week 1 of our build, large annual payments start as assets and move to expenses monthly.

title: Sale of Consigned Goods
description: Recording revenue from selling goods that belong to another vendor.
explanation: You only record your commission as revenue. The rest is a liability because you owe that money to the owner of the goods.

title: Purchase of Land with Broker Commissions
description: Capitalizing all costs required to get an asset ready for use.
explanation: Broker fees, legal costs, and title insurance are capitalized into the cost of the Land. Land is never depreciated.

title: Accruing Monthly Loan Interest
description: Recording interest that has built up on a bank loan but hasn't been paid yet.
explanation: Standard month-end adjustment to ensure your financial statements show the true cost of debt for that month.

title: Resale of Treasury Stock above Cost
description: Selling back company-owned shares to the market at a higher price than they were bought for.
explanation: You do not record a 'Gain' on the income statement when selling treasury stock; the 'profit' goes directly to Equity (APIC).

title: Monthly Social Security (SSC) Contribution
description: Recording the company's portion and the employee's deduction for Social Security in Jordan.
explanation: In Jordan, the employer pays 14.25% and the employee pays 7.5%. This entry records the total cost to the company and the liability owed to the Social Security Corporation.

title: Customer Overpayment (Credit Balance)
description: Handling a situation where a customer pays more than the invoice balance.
explanation: The extra $50 cannot be recognized as revenue yet. It is a liability (credit balance) that must be applied to a future invoice or refunded.

title: Monthly Bank Loan Repayment
description: Recording a standard loan installment consisting of both principal and interest.
explanation: Every bank payment usually splits between reducing the debt (Principal) and the cost of borrowing (Interest).

title: Influencer Barter (Product for Promotion)
description: Sending free products to an influencer in exchange for social media coverage.
explanation: Even if no cash is exchanged, you must record the cost of the inventory sent as a marketing expense to keep inventory levels accurate.

title: Unrealized Foreign Exchange Loss
description: Adjusting a foreign currency payable at year-end due to exchange rate fluctuations.
explanation: If the exchange rate changes before you pay the bill, you must revalue the liability at the year-end 'spot rate' to follow IFRS/GAAP.

title: Cash Discount on Purchase (2/10, n/30)
description: Recording the benefit of paying a vendor early to receive a cash discount.
explanation: By paying within 10 days, the company saves 2%. This reduces the cost of the inventory (perpetual system) or is recorded as a discount revenue.

title: Amortization of Trademarks/Patents
description: The monthly expense recording for the gradual expiration of an intangible asset.
explanation: Intangible assets (except Goodwill) are amortized over their useful life, similar to how physical assets are depreciated.

title: Point-of-Sale (POS) Credit Card Sale
description: Recording a store sale where the bank takes a processing fee immediately.
explanation: When a customer taps their card in-store, the bank usually deposits the net amount (Total - Fee) into your account.

title: Dividends Received from Minority Investment
description: Recording cash income from shares owned in another company.
explanation: This is non-operating income. If you own less than 20% of a company, you simply record the dividends as income when received.

title: Employee Stock-Based Compensation
description: Recognizing the monthly expense for employee stock options or RSUs.
explanation: Common in tech startups. The value of the options given to employees is recognized as an expense, but since no cash is paid, it increases Equity.

title: Cash on Delivery (COD) Collection via Courier
description: Recording the receivable from a courier (like Aramex or DHL) after they deliver a COD order.
explanation: In the Middle East, COD is huge. When the courier picks up the cash, you don't have it yet, so you move the balance from the customer to the courier's receivable account.

title: Payment of Office Security Deposit
description: Recording the refundable deposit paid to a landlord at the start of a lease.
explanation: Deposits are not expenses. They are assets because you expect to get the money back at the end of the lease, provided the property is in good condition.

title: Interest-Free Shareholder Loan
description: Recording cash injected by a business owner to cover short-term operational costs.
explanation: Common in startups and SMEs. The owner puts personal money into the company. It is a liability for the company because it eventually needs to be paid back to the owner.

title: Receipt of Client Retainer Fee
description: Recording an upfront payment from a client for future legal or consulting services.
explanation: Under the accrual basis, you haven't performed the service yet, so the money is 'unearned' and sits as a liability on the balance sheet.

title: Sale of Fixed Asset at a Loss
description: Removing an asset from the books when it is sold for less than its net book value.
explanation: The asset was worth $4,000 on the books ($10k cost - $6k dep) but sold for $2,000. The $2,000 difference is recorded as a loss on the income statement.

title: Donation of Inventory (CSR)
description: Recording the removal of stock from inventory for charitable purposes.
explanation: When you donate products, you move the 'Cost' of those products from assets to a donation expense account. Do not use the 'Sales' price.

title: Annual Employee Health Insurance Payment
description: Paying the yearly premium for staff medical insurance (Jordan/GCC).
explanation: Insurance is almost always paid upfront. You record the total as a 'Prepaid Asset' and then amortize $1,000 each month to the expense account.

title: Recording an Exempt Sale (0% VAT)
description: Recording revenue for products or services that are legally exempt from Sales Tax.
explanation: In Jordan, some items (like basic bread or certain medicines) are exempt. No Sales Tax Payable is recorded for these specific transactions.

title: Utilization of Bank Overdraft
description: Recording a negative bank balance when a company uses its credit facility for payments.
explanation: If your bank balance goes below zero, the 'Cash' asset doesn't become a negative asset—it becomes a 'Current Liability' called Bank Overdraft.

title: Reversal of Prior Month Accrual
description: Clearing out a month-end estimate at the beginning of the next period to avoid double counting.
explanation: Reversing entries are used at the start of a new month so that when the 'actual' bill arrives and is recorded, the net effect on expenses is correct.

title: Sales Discount Taken by Customer
description: Recording the reduction in cash received when a customer pays early (e.g., 2/10, n/30).
explanation: Sales Discounts is a contra-revenue account. It reduces the 'Gross Sales' to 'Net Sales' on the Income Statement.

title: Petty Cash Overage (Extra Cash Found)
description: Adjusting the petty cash fund when the physical count is higher than the records.
explanation: If the drawer has more money than the receipts show, you must increase the book balance and record the difference as 'Cash Over and Short'.

title: Employee Income Tax Withholding (Jordan)
description: Recording the deduction of income tax from an employee's gross salary based on Jordan's tax brackets.
explanation: In Jordan, employers are legally required to deduct income tax (Withholding Tax) from salaries exceeding the exempt threshold and remit it monthly to the ISTD.

title: Factoring of Accounts Receivable
description: Selling your unpaid customer invoices to a third party (Factor) to get immediate cash.
explanation: Factoring is a way to speed up cash flow. You give up a percentage of the invoice value (the fee) in exchange for getting the cash today instead of in 60 days.

title: Intercompany Expense Recharge
description: Recharging a portion of a shared expense (like office rent) from the parent company to a subsidiary.
explanation: When one entity pays a bill for the whole group, it 'recharges' the cost. This reduces the parent's expense and creates a receivable from the subsidiary.

title: Reclassification of Current Portion of Debt
description: Moving the part of a long-term loan that is due within the next 12 months to current liabilities.
explanation: At year-end, you must show investors exactly how much debt is due within a year. This doesn't change what you owe, just how it is presented on the balance sheet.

title: Customer Refund (Cash/Bank)
description: Processing a full cash refund to a customer after they returned a product.
explanation: Instead of debiting 'Sales' directly, we use 'Sales Returns' to track the volume of returns, which is a key performance indicator (KPI) for retailers.

title: Reversal of Inventory Write-Down (IFRS)
description: Increasing the value of inventory back up if its market price recovers (Not allowed under US GAAP, but allowed under IFRS).
explanation: If you wrote down stock last year because it was 'obsolete' but it suddenly becomes valuable again, IFRS allows you to reverse that loss up to the original cost.

title: Credit Card Chargeback & Fee
description: Recording a forced refund by a bank due to a customer dispute, including the penalty fee.
explanation: When a customer disputes a transaction (Stripe/PayPal), the processor pulls the original sale amount PLUS a penalty fee from your account immediately.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the estimated corporate tax on profits at the end of the fiscal year.
explanation: This entry ensures the company's net profit reflects the tax burden for the period, even if the actual payment to the tax authorities happens months later.

title: Issuing Shares for Land (Non-Cash)
description: Acquiring an asset by giving the seller company shares instead of cash.
explanation: This is a 'non-cash investing activity.' The asset is recorded at its fair market value, and equity is increased accordingly.

title: Monthly Rent Expense Recognition
description: Moving one month of rent from the prepaid asset to the expense account.
explanation: The actual cash left the bank months ago. This monthly entry ensures that each month's Profit & Loss statement shows the correct cost of occupying the office.

title: Accruing Employee Overtime
description: Recording extra hours worked by staff that will be paid in the following month's payroll.
explanation: If employees work overtime in the last week of December, the expense must be recorded in December, even if the paycheck is issued in January.

title: Project Mobilization Advance
description: Receiving an upfront payment from a client to start a construction project.
explanation: In construction, clients often pay a 'Mobilization' fee. This is a liability because if the project is cancelled before starting, the money must be returned.

title: Customs Duties & Clearing Fees on Imports
description: Capitalizing the cost of customs duties and port clearing fees into the value of imported inventory.
explanation: In Jordan, all costs required to bring inventory to your warehouse (Customs, Duties, Clearance) must be 'Capitalized'—added to the inventory cost—rather than expensed immediately.

title: Provision for Pending Lawsuit (Prudence)
description: Recording a liability for a legal case where a loss is probable and the amount can be estimated.
explanation: According to IAS 37, if your legal advisor says you will likely lose a case, you must record the expense now to protect the integrity of the financial statements.

title: Sale of Customer Gift Cards
description: Recording the cash received from selling a gift card before it is used for a purchase.
explanation: A gift card is not a sale yet. It is a liability because you 'owe' the customer products or services in the future. It only becomes revenue when they spend it.

title: Redemption of a Customer Gift Card
description: Recording revenue when a customer finally uses their gift card to buy something.
explanation: This entry converts the liability into earned revenue. The company has now fulfilled its obligation to the customer.

title: Interest on Lease Liability (IFRS 16)
description: Recording the monthly interest portion of an office lease payment.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, a lease payment is split: one part reduces the principal debt (Lease Liability) and the other part is the interest cost of financing.

title: Owner's Personal Expense Paid by Business
description: Recording a transaction where the owner used the company bank account for a personal bill.
explanation: The 'Business Entity' concept dictates that the owner and the business are separate. Personal expenses are not business expenses; they reduce the owner's equity.

title: Software Research Phase (Expensing)
description: Recording costs for the initial research phase of a new software feature.
explanation: Under IFRS/IAS 38, costs in the 'Research' phase can never be capitalized. They must be expensed immediately until the project reaches 'Technical Feasibility'.

title: Government Grant for Equipment
description: Recording a cash grant received from the government to help buy a specific machine.
explanation: Grants are usually recorded as 'Deferred Income' (a liability) and recognized as revenue over the same period the machine is depreciated.

title: Opening a Letter of Credit (L/C) Fee
description: Recording the bank fees associated with opening an L/C for international trade.
explanation: Letters of Credit are common in Jordanian imports. The fee charged by the bank to issue the L/C is an immediate finance expense.

title: Year-End Inventory Shrinkage Adjustment
description: Adjusting the books when the physical stock count is lower than the system balance.
explanation: Often caused by theft, damage, or errors. This entry ensures that the Balance Sheet reflects the actual physical items currently in the warehouse.

title: Capitalizing Interest on Construction Loan
description: Adding the interest cost of a project loan to the cost of the building being constructed (IAS 23).
explanation: If you borrow money to build a 'Qualifying Asset' (like a factory), you don't expense the interest. You add it to the cost of the building until it's finished.

title: Recovery of Written-Off Debt (Direct Reversal)
description: Reinstating a customer account and recording the cash received after a direct write-off occurred.
explanation: This is a two-step process: First, you reverse the write-off to bring back the 'audit trail' of the customer, then you record the cash collection.

title: Biological Asset Fair Value Increase
description: Adjusting the value of livestock or crops as they grow or market prices change (IAS 41).
explanation: In agriculture, assets like sheep or olive trees are measured at fair value. As they grow or become more valuable, the increase is recorded as income even before they are sold.

title: Correction of Prior Period Accounting Error
description: Adjusting for an expense that was completely forgotten in the previous fiscal year.
explanation: If you find a mistake from a closed year, you cannot record the expense in the current year's P&L. You must adjust the 'Retained Earnings' directly to show the correction of history.

title: Lease Incentive (Rent-Free Period)
description: Accounting for a 12-month lease where the first 2 months are free.
explanation: To follow the accrual basis, you must spread the total cost of the lease over the whole year. Even in 'free' months, you record a portion of the expense.

title: Insurance Claim Settlement Received
description: Recording the cash received from an insurance company after a fire or theft loss.
explanation: This entry offsets the loss previously recorded. If the insurance payout is higher than the book value of the lost asset, the difference is 'Gain on Insurance Settlement'.

title: Declaration of Stock Dividends (Bonus Shares)
description: Issuing new shares to existing shareholders instead of cash.
explanation: A stock dividend doesn't change the company's total equity, but it moves money from Retained Earnings into the Share Capital account.

title: Government Fines & Penalties (Non-Deductible)
description: Recording a traffic fine or late filing penalty that cannot be used to reduce taxes.
explanation: While this is a business expense, in Jordan and KSA, fines are usually 'disallowed' for tax purposes. You record it now but add it back when calculating taxable income.

title: Accruing Unbilled Revenue (Work-in-Progress)
description: Recording revenue for work performed this month that hasn't been officially invoiced yet.
explanation: Common in law and consulting firms. If you worked 20 hours in December but bill in January, you must recognize the revenue in December to match the effort.

title: Purchase Price Variance (PPV)
description: Recording the difference between the standard cost of a material and the actual price paid.
explanation: Used in standard costing systems. This highlights to management that materials are getting more expensive than the budget (Standard Cost) predicted.

title: Employee Travel Allowance (Per Diem)
description: Paying a fixed daily rate to an employee for business travel expenses.
explanation: Instead of collecting every small receipt, many companies pay a flat 'Per Diem' rate. This is a straightforward business expense.

title: Capitalizing a Major Asset Overhaul
description: Recording a significant repair that extends the life of a machine by several years.
explanation: Regular maintenance (oil changes) is expensed. But a major engine replacement that makes the machine last 5 years longer is 'Capitalized'—added to the asset value.

title: Funds Held in Escrow (Property Sale)
description: Recording cash held by a third party during a property transaction.
explanation: In Real Estate, money is often held in a neutral account until the legal transfer of the title is complete. It is restricted cash, not yet revenue.

title: Reversal of Excess Bad Debt Provision
description: Reducing the allowance for doubtful accounts when collections are better than expected.
explanation: If you estimated a $5,000 loss last year but now realize you only expect $4,000, you 'reverse' the $1,000 to increase your current year profit.

title: Initial Partnership Capital Contribution
description: Recording the initial cash and equipment investment by two partners to start a new business.
explanation: In a partnership, equity is split into individual capital accounts for each partner to track their specific ownership stake and share of profits.

title: Cash Margin for Bank Guarantee (LG)
description: Recording the cash frozen by the bank as collateral for issuing a Letter of Guarantee (common in Jordan tenders).
explanation: When a bank issues a guarantee (LG) for a project, they often 'block' a percentage of the amount in your account. This is still your asset, but it is 'Restricted' and cannot be spent.

title: Purchase of Bitcoin/Crypto for Treasury
description: Recording the acquisition of digital assets as a long-term investment.
explanation: Under current IFRS, cryptocurrencies are generally treated as 'Intangible Assets' rather than cash or financial instruments, unless held for sale in the ordinary course of business.

title: Accruing Affiliate Marketing Commissions
description: Recording the commission owed to influencers or partners for sales made through their links.
explanation: To match expenses with the revenue they generated, you must record the commission cost in the same month the sale happened, even if you pay the affiliate 30 days later.

title: Recognition of Deferred Tax Liability
description: Accounting for the timing difference between accounting depreciation and tax depreciation (Advanced).
explanation: If the tax office allows you to depreciate an asset faster than your books do, you pay less tax today but will pay more later. This 'future tax' is recorded as a Deferred Tax Liability.

title: Recruitment Agency Success Fee
description: Recording the fee paid to a headhunter after successfully hiring a new manager.
explanation: Agency fees are operating expenses. They are usually calculated as a percentage (e.g., 15-20%) of the new hire's annual starting salary.

title: Barter Exchange (Services for Services)
description: Recording a transaction where you provide marketing services in exchange for legal advice (no cash involved).
explanation: Even if no money moves, you must record the Fair Market Value of the services. This ensures your total revenue and total expenses are accurate for tax purposes.

title: Accrued Interest on Bank Term Deposit
description: Recording interest earned on a 6-month fixed deposit that hasn't been paid into the account yet.
explanation: Standard month-end entry. Even if the bank only pays the interest at the end of the 6 months, you must 'earn' a portion of it every month on your books.

title: Release of Restricted NGO Funds
description: Moving funds from 'Restricted' to 'Unrestricted' revenue after project milestones are met.
explanation: In Non-Profit accounting, once you spend money on the specific purpose the donor requested, you 'release' that amount from restriction to show it has been used.

title: Scrapping a Fully Depreciated Asset
description: Removing an asset and its accumulated depreciation from the books when it is thrown away (Zero book value).
explanation: If an asset is fully depreciated (Cost = Accum. Depr.) and you throw it away, there is no gain or loss. You simply 'flush' both accounts to zero to keep your asset register clean.

title: Revaluation of Investment Property (Fair Value)
description: Adjusting the value of a building held for rental income to its current market price (IAS 40).
explanation: Under the IAS 40 Fair Value model, investment properties are not depreciated. Instead, any change in market value goes directly to the Income Statement each year.

title: Stock Split (Memo Entry Only)
description: A 2-for-1 stock split where the number of shares doubles and par value halves.
explanation: A stock split does not require a formal journal entry because the total dollar value of equity remains the same. You only update the share count and par value in the records.

title: Rent Payment with Withholding Tax (Jordan)
description: Recording a rent payment to an individual landlord where 5% tax is deducted at source.
explanation: In Jordan, when a company rents from an individual, it must withhold 5% of the rent and pay it to the Income & Sales Tax Department (ISTD) on behalf of the landlord.

title: Inventory in Transit (FOB Shipping Point)
description: Recording inventory that is on the way but legally owned by the company during transit.
explanation: If the shipping terms are 'FOB Shipping Point', the buyer owns the goods as soon as they leave the seller's warehouse. You must record them even if they haven't arrived at your dock yet.

title: Retention Payable to Subcontractor
description: Withholding a percentage of a subcontractor's payment as a guarantee for their work quality.
explanation: Common in Jordan's construction sector. You pay the sub 90% and keep 10% (Retention) until the 'Defects Liability Period' is over, usually one year later.

title: Trademark & Domain Registration
description: Capitalizing the legal and registration costs of a new brand or web domain.
explanation: Domain names and trademarks that have a long-term benefit for the company are recorded as intangible assets rather than being expensed immediately.

title: Employee Loan Repayment via Payroll
description: Recording the monthly deduction from an employee's salary to pay back a company loan.
explanation: This entry reduces the liability the company has to the employee (their salary) and simultaneously reduces the asset (the loan the employee owes the company).

title: Monthly Cloud Hosting (AWS/Azure)
description: Recording the pay-as-you-go expense for server infrastructure.
explanation: Unlike annual licenses, monthly 'utility-style' cloud billing is expensed immediately in the period the usage occurred.

title: Resale of Treasury Stock at a Loss
description: Selling company-owned shares back to the market for less than what they were bought for.
explanation: If you sell treasury stock at a loss, you first reduce any existing 'Paid-in Capital from Treasury Stock'. If that's zero, you reduce Retained Earnings. You never record a loss on the Income Statement.

title: Cash Register Shortage
description: Adjusting for a discrepancy when the physical cash in the drawer is less than the POS report.
explanation: A common retail entry. Small shortages are expensed. Large, recurring shortages usually trigger an internal audit for potential theft or error.

title: Dividend Payment with Withholding Tax
description: Paying a dividend to shareholders and withholding the required government tax (Common in KSA/GCC).
explanation: Many jurisdictions require companies to withhold a percentage of dividends as tax before sending the net amount to the shareholder.

title: Bargain Purchase Gain (Negative Goodwill)
description: Recording the gain when a business is acquired for less than the fair value of its net assets.
explanation: Under IFRS 3, if you buy a company for less than its actual worth, the 'profit' is recognized immediately on the Income Statement as a gain.

title: Sale of Inbound Consignment Goods
description: Selling an item that belongs to a vendor, where you only act as the agent.
explanation: You don't own the inventory. When it sells, you create a liability to the owner for their share and recognize your commission as revenue.

title: Fair Value of FX Forward Contract
description: Adjusting the value of a hedge contract at month-end based on market exchange rates.
explanation: If you use a Forward Contract to lock in a USD rate for an import, IFRS 9 requires you to record the 'value' of that contract as it changes each month.

title: Recording App Store Sales (Net of 30% Fee)
description: How to record revenue from Apple (App Store) or Google Play when they deduct their commission before payout.
explanation: Even though you only receive $70, you must record the full $100 as revenue and $30 as an expense. Recording only $70 as revenue would be incorrect for tax and growth analysis.

title: Purchase of Raw Materials on Credit
description: Recording the arrival of raw materials (steel, plastic, etc.) to the warehouse.
explanation: Raw materials are assets, not expenses. They only become an expense (COGS) once they are converted into a finished product and sold to a customer.

title: Allocating Direct Labor to Production
description: Moving factory worker wages from the payroll account into Work-in-Process (WIP).
explanation: In manufacturing, factory labor is 'productized.' The cost is added to the value of the inventory sitting on the assembly line.

title: Accruing a Vendor Volume Rebate
description: Recording a 'cash back' or credit note earned from a supplier for hitting a purchase target.
explanation: Rebates reduce the cost of your inventory. If you've already sold the items, the rebate reduces your COGS, thereby increasing your gross profit.

title: End of Service Benefit (EOSB) Payout
description: Recording the actual cash payment to an employee who has resigned or been terminated.
explanation: This entry clears the liability that the company had been building up over the years. No 'expense' is recorded now because it was already expensed monthly via provisions.

title: Receipt of VAT Refund from Government
description: Recording the cash received from the ISTD (Jordan) after a successful tax refund claim.
explanation: When the government pays you back for excess input tax, you move the balance from the 'Refundable' asset account to your actual bank account.

title: Impairment of Intangible Asset (Trademark)
description: Writing down the value of a brand name that has lost its market relevance or legal protection.
explanation: If a brand you bought becomes worthless (e.g., a failed product line), you must remove its value from the balance sheet immediately.

title: Retirement of Treasury Stock
description: Formally canceling shares that the company bought back, reducing the total share count permanently.
explanation: Retirement flushes the Treasury Stock account and reduces the Common Stock and APIC accounts to reflect that those shares no longer exist.

title: Free Trial / Freemium Server Costs
description: Allocating the hosting costs of 'free' users to marketing/acquisition expense.
explanation: In many SaaS models, the cost of supporting free users is treated as a marketing cost rather than a cost of service for paying customers.

title: Physical Disposal of Obsolete Inventory
description: Removing items from the warehouse that were previously 'reserved' for obsolescence.
explanation: This entry happens when you actually throw the items in the trash. It clears both the inventory account and the provision (allowance) account you created earlier.

title: Employee Training & Certification Cost
description: Paying for an employee's professional exam (e.g., CPA/CMA) or training course.
explanation: Training is an operating expense. While it 'improves' the employee, it is generally not capitalized as an asset because the company doesn't 'own' the employee's knowledge.

title: Donating a Fixed Asset (e.g., Laptop) to Charity
description: Removing an old company laptop from the books and recording a donation expense.
explanation: The donation expense is recorded at the 'Net Book Value' of the asset at the time of the gift (Cost minus Depreciation).

title: Capitalizing Internal Labor for Fixed Assets
description: Moving internal staff wages from expenses to a Fixed Asset account because they built a company-used machine or facility.
explanation: If your own engineers build a machine for the factory, their salary for that time is not an 'operating expense'—it is part of the cost of the asset and must be capitalized.

title: Gain on Early Settlement of Debt
description: Paying off a loan early for a discounted amount and recording the resulting gain.
explanation: If a lender agrees to accept $9,500 to close a $10,000 debt, the $500 difference is a gain that must be reported on the income statement.

title: Post-Paid API Usage Costs (OpenAI/Twilio)
description: Accruing the expense for API credits used during the month before the bill is actually paid.
explanation: For consumption-based models, you calculate your month-end usage and record the expense immediately to ensure your margins are accurate for that specific month.

title: Real Estate Brokerage Commission
description: Recording the commission paid to an agent for successfully selling a property.
explanation: Commissions are usually a percentage of the sale price. They are recorded as a selling expense in the period the sale is finalized.

title: Allocating Costs to Joint Products
description: Splitting common production costs between two different products created from the same process.
explanation: Common in industries like oil refining or dairy. Total costs are split based on the 'Sales Value at Split-off' or physical quantity of the resulting products.

title: Reversal of Overestimated Audit Fees
description: Adjusting the books when the actual audit bill is lower than the amount previously accrued.
explanation: If you accrued $5,000 but the auditor only charged $4,500, you reduce the expense in the current period to correct the prior estimate.

title: Property Dividend (Dividend in Kind)
description: Distributing a physical asset (like equipment or inventory) to shareholders instead of cash.
explanation: The asset is usually revalued to fair market value first, then distributed. It reduces the company's equity and its asset base simultaneously.

title: Factoring AR without Recourse
description: Selling receivables where the buyer (factor) assumes all risk of the customer not paying.
explanation: Because it is 'Without Recourse,' the company has no further liability. It is treated as a final sale of an asset rather than a loan.

title: Issuing a Subscription Credit Note
description: Granting a customer a credit for a 'paused' month or as a customer service gesture.
explanation: Credits reduce the amount the customer owes or the amount of revenue you are allowed to recognize in the future.

title: Employee Severance / Termination Pay
description: Recording a one-time lump sum payment for a redundant position (separate from EOSB).
explanation: Unlike monthly EOSB provisions, severance for redundancy is often an unexpected, one-time operating expense.

title: Cumulative Translation Adjustment (CTA)
description: Recording the change in equity due to translating a foreign branch's books into the home currency.
explanation: When a Jordanian company has a branch in the UK (GBP), the change in value of that branch due only to exchange rates is recorded in 'Other Comprehensive Income' (OCI).

title: Imputed Interest on Zero-Interest Loan
description: Recording the 'theoretical' interest on a long-term interest-free loan to a related party.
explanation: Accounting standards (IFRS 9) require that loans be recorded at 'Fair Value.' If a loan has 0% interest, you must 'impute' an interest rate to reflect the time value of money.

title: VAT Reverse Charge on Imported Services
description: Accounting for VAT on services purchased from a foreign vendor (e.g., Google Ads or a consultant in Europe).
explanation: In Jordan and the GCC, when you buy services from abroad, you 'self-assess' the tax. You record both an Input and Output VAT, which usually nets to zero but must be shown on the tax return.

title: Hotel Room No-Show Fee
description: Recording revenue when a guest fails to arrive and their credit card is charged for the first night.
explanation: Since no service (stay) was provided but the room was held, the cancellation fee is recognized as non-operating or other room revenue immediately.

title: Expected Credit Loss (ECL) Provision
description: Recording a provision for bad debts based on forward-looking probability (IFRS 9 requirement).
explanation: Unlike the 'old' bad debt method, IFRS 9 requires companies to estimate potential losses on all receivables, even those not yet overdue, based on economic trends.

title: Customer Loyalty Points (Revenue Deferral)
description: Allocating a portion of a sale's price to the loyalty points earned by the customer.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, you cannot recognize the full $100 as revenue if the points have a value. You must 'defer' the portion representing the points until they are redeemed or expire.

title: Initial Recognition: Finance Lease (Lessor)
description: Recording the removal of an asset and the recognition of a lease receivable when leasing out equipment.
explanation: If a company (Lessor) transfers all risks/rewards of an asset to a tenant, they remove the asset from their books and replace it with a receivable for the future payments.

title: Cash Flow Hedge: Fuel/Commodity
description: Recording the change in fair value of a fuel price hedge contract (Derivative).
explanation: Large logistics companies 'lock in' fuel prices. If the market price rises, the contract becomes more valuable. The gain is stored in 'Other Comprehensive Income' (OCI) until the fuel is actually bought.

title: Issuance of Stock Warrants
description: Recording the cash received from selling warrants (the right to buy stock at a fixed price later).
explanation: Warrants are equity instruments. The cash received is added to shareholders' equity. No liability is created because the company just 'owes' shares, not cash.

title: Actual Payment of Zakat (Saudi/GCC)
description: The cash outflow when paying the annual Zakat liability to the tax authority (ZATCA).
explanation: This follows the 'Zakat Provision' entry. It clears the liability from the balance sheet and records the actual cash payment.

title: Recovery of Inventory Write-Down (IFRS)
description: Increasing the value of inventory back to its original cost because the market price recovered.
explanation: Under IFRS, if you wrote down stock due to a price drop but the price goes back up, you must 'reverse' the loss. Note: This is NOT allowed under US GAAP.

title: Asset Exchange (Equipment for Vehicle)
description: Trading an old machine for a company car, including a cash adjustment.
explanation: To record an exchange, you remove the old asset and its depreciation, record the new asset at its fair value, and balance the difference as a gain, loss, or cash payment.

title: Capitalizing Software Development Costs
description: Moving internal coding costs from expenses to assets once technical feasibility is met (IAS 38).
explanation: For SaaS companies, once a product is 'viable,' the salaries of the developers are no longer expensed—they are treated as an asset and amortized over the product's life.

title: Charitable Pledge Accrual
description: Recording a legal commitment to donate money to a charity before the cash is sent.
explanation: If a company publicly announces or signs a contract to donate, the expense is recognized immediately under the accrual basis, even if the check is written next year.

title: Semester Tuition Revenue Recognition
description: Recording the monthly portion of tuition revenue from a previously collected semester fee.
explanation: Schools often collect fees for 4–6 months upfront. To follow the matching principle, you only recognize 1/4th or 1/6th of that money as revenue each month.

title: Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) Accrual
description: Recording the future cost of dismantling a specialized facility (like an oil tank or factory) at the end of its life.
explanation: If the law requires you to 'clean up' or restore a site after 20 years, you must capitalize that future cost into the asset today and depreciate it over the asset's life.

title: Insurance Claim Rejection (Partial Write-off)
description: Adjusting a medical receivable when an insurance company refuses to pay the full billed amount.
explanation: In healthcare, 'Gross Charges' are rarely paid in full. The difference between what you bill and what the insurer pays is a 'Contractual Adjustment' (not bad debt).

title: Capitalizing Interest on Project Loans
description: Adding interest costs to the value of a 'qualifying asset' during its construction period (IAS 23).
explanation: If you borrow money specifically to build a factory, the interest paid *during* construction is not an expense—it is part of the cost of the factory.

title: Sale-Leaseback Transaction (Gain Recognition)
description: Selling an office building and immediately leasing it back from the buyer to free up cash.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, a sale-leaseback is only a 'sale' if it meets revenue standards. The gain is often restricted to the portion of the rights transferred to the buyer-lessor.

title: Research & Development (R&D) Tax Credit
description: Recording a government tax rebate for qualifying innovation and development costs.
explanation: R&D credits reduce your tax liability. When received, they can either reduce your tax expense or be recorded as 'Other Income' depending on the local jurisdiction.

title: Outbound Consignment Shipment
description: Moving inventory from your main warehouse to a dealer's showroom while still retaining ownership.
explanation: No sale has occurred. This is a 'transfer' entry that keeps track of where your physical stock is located until the dealer actually sells it.

title: Transfer: Inventory to Investment Property
description: Moving a completed apartment from 'For Sale' (Inventory) to 'For Rent' (Investment Property).
explanation: If you decide to rent out a unit you originally built to sell, IAS 40 requires you to move it to Investment Property. Any jump to 'Fair Value' at that moment is recorded as a gain.

title: Early Retirement of Bonds Payable (at Loss)
description: Buying back company bonds on the open market for more than their carrying value.
explanation: If interest rates drop, a company might pay a 'premium' to retire old debt early. The difference between the cash paid and the debt's book value is an immediate loss.

title: Monthly Subscription Box (Physical Goods)
description: Recognizing revenue for a 'Snack Box' or 'Beauty Box' once it is shipped to the subscriber.
explanation: Unlike SaaS, physical subscriptions must recognize both the revenue and the COGS at the moment the physical goods leave the warehouse.

title: Receipt of Legal Settlement (Gain)
description: Recording cash received from winning a lawsuit against a competitor.
explanation: Accounting is conservative: you record legal liabilities early (provisions), but you only record legal gains when the cash is virtually certain or received.

title: Distribution of Dividends-in-Kind
description: Giving shareholders company inventory or property instead of a cash dividend.
explanation: Common in small private companies. The 'value' of the dividend is usually the Fair Market Value of the assets being handed over.

title: Monthly Accrual for Employee Vacation
description: Recording the liability for earned but unused vacation days at month-end.
explanation: To follow the matching principle, the cost of an employee's vacation must be recorded as they 'earn' it each month, not just when they actually take the time off.

title: Bill-and-Hold Sale Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue when the customer is billed and title passes, but the company keeps the goods in its warehouse at the customer's request.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, if the customer controls the goods and has requested the delay in delivery, you can recognize the sale even if the items haven't physically moved.

title: Revenue Recognition: Acting as Agent (Net)
description: Recording revenue for a marketplace sale where the company only keeps a commission (Net Basis).
explanation: If you don't control the goods and aren't responsible for fulfillment, you are an 'Agent.' You must only record your fee ($15) as revenue, not the total transaction value ($100).

title: Interest Charged on Overdue Customer Accounts
description: Recording a finance charge applied to a customer's balance because they paid past the due date.
explanation: This increases the amount the customer owes and recognizes the interest as other operating income.

title: Write-off of Obsolete Internal Software
description: Removing the remaining book value of a software tool that is no longer being used by the company.
explanation: If a software asset has no future economic benefit (it’s obsolete), you must 'flush' the cost and accumulated amortization to zero and record the loss.

title: Sale of Production Byproduct
description: Recording revenue from selling a secondary product that is created automatically during the main manufacturing process.
explanation: Byproducts (like sawdust in a lumber mill) are usually recorded as 'Other Income' or used to reduce the cost of the main product.

title: Annual IT Support/Maintenance Contract
description: Paying upfront for a 1-year technical support or hardware maintenance agreement.
explanation: This payment covers 12 months of service. Each month, you will move $100 from 'Prepaid' to 'Maintenance Expense'.

title: Change in Asset Useful Life Estimate
description: Adjusting the monthly depreciation because a machine is now expected to last longer than originally planned.
explanation: A change in estimate is 'Prospective.' You don't fix the past; you just calculate the remaining book value and spread it over the new remaining years.

title: Advance Payment to Foreign Supplier (USD)
description: Recording a cash advance to a foreign vendor in USD, translated to local currency (e.g., JOD).
explanation: Even though you pay in USD, the entry must be recorded in your 'Functional Currency' (JOD) using the spot exchange rate on the day of payment.

title: Interest Income on Intercompany Loan
description: Recording interest earned on money lent to a subsidiary or sister company.
explanation: To ensure 'Arm's Length' transactions, parent companies should charge interest to subsidiaries. This creates a receivable for the parent and a revenue stream.

title: Redemption of Store Discount Coupon
description: Recording a sale where the customer uses a $10 discount coupon.
explanation: This allows the business to track exactly how much revenue was 'lost' to marketing promotions versus actual gross sales.

title: Professional Indemnity Insurance Premium
description: Paying for insurance that covers legal liability for professional errors or omissions.
explanation: Common for auditors, architects, and consultants. It is treated as a prepaid asset and expensed over the 1-year coverage period.

title: Equity Method: Recording Share of Associate Profit
description: Recording the company's share of net income from an associate company (20-50% ownership) under IAS 28.
explanation: When you own a significant stake (but not control), you don't record dividends as income. Instead, you increase the value of your investment by your share of their profit.

title: Gift Card 'Breakage' (Unredeemed Revenue)
description: Recognizing revenue from gift cards that are highly unlikely to ever be redeemed based on historical data.
explanation: If statistics show that 5% of gift cards are never used, you can 'bleed' that liability into revenue over time. This is known as 'breakage' in retail accounting.

title: Replenishing Fleet Fuel Cards
description: Recording the top-up of prepaid fuel cards (like Total or JoPetrol) for a fleet of delivery trucks.
explanation: Fuel cards are essentially 'prepaid assets.' You only move the amount to 'Fuel Expense' when the drivers actually swipe the cards at the station.

title: Deferred Tax Asset (Tax Loss Carryforward)
description: Recognizing a tax asset because the company had a loss this year that can reduce taxes in future profitable years.
explanation: If you lose money today, you get a 'credit' for the future. You record this as an asset if it is probable that you will have future profits to use the credit against.

title: Complimentary Meals for Influencers
description: Recording the cost of food provided for free to food bloggers or influencers for marketing purposes.
explanation: You don't record the 'menu price' as a sale. You simply move the cost of the raw ingredients out of inventory and into a marketing expense account.

title: Bank Overdraft Interest & Fees
description: Recording the finance charges applied by the bank for using an overdraft facility.
explanation: Overdrafts are short-term loans. The interest charged by the bank must be accrued monthly, even if it's automatically deducted from the account.

title: Partial Impairment of Goodwill
description: Writing down a portion of Goodwill because a subsidiary's fair value has decreased (IAS 36).
explanation: Goodwill is tested annually. If the business unit is worth less than its carrying amount, the excess must be written off as a permanent loss. You cannot reverse this later.

title: Receipt of Advance Success Fee
description: Recording a deposit for a 'Success Fee' (e.g., in M&A or Legal) that is only earned if a specific outcome is achieved.
explanation: Since the 'success' hasn't happened yet, the money is a liability. It only moves to revenue once the deal is closed or the case is won.

title: Influencer Samples (Product Seedings)
description: Recording the cost of shipping free products to influencers for review (No sale).
explanation: Similar to the restaurant entry, this clears the physical stock from the warehouse and attributes the cost to marketing efforts.

title: Provision for Onerous Contract (Expected Loss)
description: Recording an immediate loss when it becomes clear that the cost of a construction project will exceed the revenue.
explanation: According to the Principle of Conservatism, if you know a project will lose money, you must record the entire expected loss immediately, even if the project is not finished.

title: Monthly Amortization of Reserved Instances
description: Monthly adjustment for an upfront 1-year 'Reserved Instance' payment for AWS or Google Cloud.
explanation: Large SaaS companies pay upfront for 'Reserved Instances' to save money. This entry moves 1/12th of that cost to the monthly P&L.

title: Correction of Bank Deposit Error
description: Adjusting the books when the bank incorrectly recorded a $1,000 deposit as $100.
explanation: Found during bank reconciliation. You must increase the book balance to match the actual cash while the bank processes the correction.

title: Issuance of a Convertible Note
description: Recording cash received from an investor via a convertible debt instrument (common in tech startups).
explanation: At the start, a convertible note is recorded as debt. It only moves to Equity (Shares) later during a 'Conversion Event' or a new funding round.

title: Purchase of Carbon Credits (Offsets)
description: Recording the cost of buying carbon credits to offset the company's environmental footprint.
explanation: Carbon offsets are typically expensed as part of a company's CSR or sustainability initiatives. If held for trading, they would be recorded as an intangible asset.

title: Prorated Subscription Upgrade
description: Recording the additional revenue when a customer moves from a Basic to a Premium plan mid-month.
explanation: This handles the 'gap' between the old price and the new price for the remaining days of the billing cycle. It is recognized as revenue immediately since the service is active.

title: Monthly Accrual for Annual Property Tax
description: Estimating and recording the monthly portion of an annual property tax bill for an office or warehouse.
explanation: Even if the government only bills you once a year, the cost 'belongs' to every month. Accruing it monthly prevents a huge profit drop in the month the bill actually arrives.

title: Event Ticket Sales (Gross vs Net)
description: Recording ticket sales revenue where the platform (like Eventbrite) takes a fee before payout.
explanation: Always record the 'Gross' ticket price as revenue. The fees are a marketing or operational expense. This ensures your sales data matches your pricing.

title: Employee Relocation Allowance
description: Paying for a new hire's moving costs and flights to join the company.
explanation: Relocation costs are treated as an operating expense. In some regions, this may be a taxable benefit for the employee, requiring a separate payroll entry.

title: Initial Franchise Fee Payment
description: Paying the one-time upfront fee to a franchisor to open a new location (e.g., McDonald's or Starbucks sub-franchise).
explanation: Initial fees are capitalized as an intangible asset and amortized over the life of the franchise agreement (e.g., 10 years).

title: Return of Defective Goods to Supplier
description: Recording a credit note from a supplier after returning damaged inventory.
explanation: This reduces the balance you owe the vendor and corrects your inventory count. No expense is involved; it's a balance sheet adjustment.

title: Government Solar Energy Incentive
description: Recording a tax credit or cash grant received for installing renewable energy systems.
explanation: Under IAS 20, government grants for assets are either recorded as deferred income or used to reduce the carrying amount of the asset (Solar Panels).

title: Warehouse Pallet Rental Expense
description: Recording the monthly cost of renting wooden/plastic pallets for storage.
explanation: Many logistics companies rent pallets rather than buying them. These are recurring operating expenses recorded every month.

title: Royalty Income from IP License
description: Receiving a monthly payment from another company for using your patented technology or brand.
explanation: Royalties are typically earned based on the licensee's sales. This is a common revenue stream for tech-heavy companies and designers.

title: Fixed Asset Revaluation (Downward/Loss)
description: Recording a decrease in land/building value below its original cost (IFRS).
explanation: Unlike revaluation gains (which go to Equity/OCI), a loss that drops the asset below its historical cost must be recorded on the Income Statement immediately.

title: Port Demurrage & Detention Fees
description: Recording the penalty fees paid to a shipping line for delayed container return (common in Aqaba/Jebel Ali).
explanation: If a container stays at the port longer than the 'free days' allowed, the company pays demurrage. This is an operating expense and is often non-refundable.

title: Capitalizing Leasehold Improvements
description: Recording the cost of permanent renovations (flooring, walls) made to a rented office space.
explanation: Renovations to a rented space are capitalized as an asset and depreciated over the shorter of the lease term or the improvement's useful life.

title: Closing Under-applied Overhead
description: Adjusting Cost of Goods Sold when actual factory overhead was higher than the amount 'applied' to products.
explanation: At year-end, if your factory cost more to run than you estimated (under-applied), you must 'charge' that extra cost to COGS to balance the books.

title: Purchase of Bond Investment at Discount
description: Recording the purchase of a government or corporate bond for less than its face value.
explanation: The bond is recorded at its cost. Over time, the $500 discount will be 'amortized' into interest income until the bond reaches its full face value at maturity.

title: Prorated Refund for Churned Customer
description: Issuing a partial refund to a customer who cancelled their annual plan halfway through.
explanation: Since the revenue was 'Deferred' (unearned), the refund simply reduces that liability. No 'Revenue' account is hit because the money was never recognized as earned.

title: Stock Option (ESOP) Vesting Expense
description: Recording the non-cash compensation expense as employee stock options vest over time.
explanation: Even though no cash is paid, IFRS 2/ASC 718 requires companies to expense the 'Fair Value' of options given to employees as they earn them.

title: Performance Bond Insurance Premium
description: Paying for a bond that guarantees the client that the project will be completed.
explanation: Performance bonds are mandatory for many government tenders in Jordan/KSA. The premium paid to the insurance company is an immediate project cost.

title: Settlement Payout to Consignor
description: Paying a vendor for the goods they owned that were sold in your store last month.
explanation: This is the final step in the consignment cycle: sending the vendor their share of the sales (Total Sales minus your Commission).

title: Bank Loan Processing/Origination Fee
description: Recording the upfront fee charged by a bank to set up a new commercial loan.
explanation: Loan fees are usually not expensed immediately. They are deducted from the loan balance and 'spread' (amortized) over the life of the loan as interest expense.

title: Medical Supplies Expiry/Wastage
description: Adjusting pharmacy or clinic inventory for medicine that has passed its expiration date.
explanation: In healthcare, tracking expiry is critical. Expired items are written off to ensure the asset value on the balance sheet is not overstated.

title: Affiliate Commission Payout
description: Making the actual cash payment to an influencer for their accumulated sales commissions.
explanation: This clears the liability created during the sale. The expense was already recorded when the sale happened; now we are just settling the debt.

title: Impairment of Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset
description: Writing down the value of a leased office or equipment if its utility has decreased (e.g., leaving an office early).
explanation: If a leased space is no longer useful (common during downsizes), you must test the ROU asset for impairment and write it down to its recoverable amount.

title: Recording Expenses Against Restricted Grant
description: Matching project expenses to the restricted funding received from a donor (Matching Principle in NGOs).
explanation: In NGO accounting, you often only 'earn' the revenue as you spend the money on the project. This double entry records the cost and simultaneously 'releases' the donor funds from the balance sheet to the income statement.

title: Contract Change Order (Increased Scope)
description: Adjusting the contract value and revenue when a client requests additional work mid-project.
explanation: When a change order is signed, the total 'Contract Price' increases. This entry recognizes the revenue for the additional progress made on that new scope of work.

title: User Referral Credit (Non-Cash)
description: Recording a $20 credit given to an existing user for referring a friend, to be used on their next bill.
explanation: Even though no cash was paid, the company now 'owes' the user a discount. This is a marketing expense that creates a liability until the user applies the credit to a future invoice.

title: Mystery Shopper Fee & Reimbursement
description: Recording the cost of an audit where a 'mystery shopper' is paid a fee plus the cost of the goods they bought.
explanation: The entire cost (the shopper's fee and the products they 'purchased' for the test) is treated as a Market Research or Quality Control expense.

title: Rent Collected on Behalf of Owner (Agency)
description: How a property management company records rent received from a tenant that belongs to the landlord.
explanation: As an agent, only your commission (e.g., 10%) is revenue. The rest is a liability because you must transfer that money to the property owner.

title: Sale of Production Scrap (By-product)
description: Recording the sale of metal shavings or waste wood from the factory floor.
explanation: Small amounts of waste material sold are typically recorded as 'Other Income'. If the amount is massive, it can be used to reduce the Cost of Goods Sold for the main product.

title: Amortization of Bond Premium
description: Reducing the Bond Premium liability over time to decrease the effective interest expense.
explanation: Because the bond was sold at a premium (above face value), the 'real' interest expense is lower than the actual cash paid to bondholders. The premium is 'bled' out over the bond's life.

title: VAT Audit Penalty (Non-Deductible)
description: Recording a penalty issued by the ISTD (Jordan) for an error in a previous VAT filing.
explanation: Fines and penalties are business expenses for accounting, but they are 'Non-Deductible' for tax. This means you record the loss now, but you can't use it to lower your income tax bill later.

title: Petty Cash Theft / Unexplained Shortage
